data.title: Bulls In A China Shop: 5 Chinese Stocks To Ride Out The Trade War
data.description:
Has China Taken the Upper Hand in Trump’s Trade War?

As Washington and Beijing remain locked in a power struggle over tariffs, Donald Trump has maintained that his decision to exempt certain high-tech Chinese exports from reciprocal tariffs was by no means a sign of surrender to Xi Jinping’s government.

"NOBODY is getting ‘off the hook’ for the unfair Trade Balances, and Non-Monetary Tariff Barriers, that other Countries have used against us, especially not China which, by far, treats us the worst! There was no Tariff "exception" announced on Friday," Trump wrote on his Social Truth platform.

Trump’s 145% tariffs on China will undoubtedly inflict major pain. Approximately 15% of the country’s exports went to the U.S. last year, amounting to a trade surplus of nearly $300B. However, China holds one major advantage in its fast-escalating trade war with the U.S.: China can more easily replace its imports from the U.S. than vice versa.

An analysis by The Financial Times of data from the Harvard Atlas of Economic Complexity showed that US exports to China were more agriculture-focused, including soybeans, cotton, beef and poultry. Most U.S. imports from China were more heavily composed of electronics, technology, and processed minerals.

While China will suffer a hit from Trump’s tariffs (the country relies on the U.S. for key semiconductor components and pharmaceuticals, to name a few), its exports are harder to replicate Stateside. China has access to rare minerals such as dysprosium and terbium, essential manufacturing components for electric vehicles. Reducing the U.S.’s dependence on China-made electronics and machinery, while not impossible, would be a long and arduous process.

“U.S. dependence on China is higher because China can source agricultural products from elsewhere more easily than the U.S. can replace electronics and machinery,” Marta Bengoa, professor of international economics at the City University of New York, told the FT. “Beijing is already buying up soybeans from Brazil, for example, so in the end China has a bit more leverage.”

Meanwhile, a weakening U.S. dollar has strengthened China’s economic prowess and made it more expensive for the U.S. to import its goods while also pushing American investors into foreign markets. Data released this week revealed that China’s export growth surged to a four-month high in March—far above expectations—illustrating the dominance of Chinese exports and ramping up bullishness on China despite tariff headwinds.

How the trade war pans out between the two nations is still anyone’s guess, as Trump and Xi continue to retaliate against the other’s ever-changing demands. From an economic perspective, however, it appears that China has the tools to come out of this trade war in a better position than the U.S.—though neither will emerge unscathed.

China’s ‘Biggest Losers’ Primed for Big Wins

The sell-off triggered by tariffs has shaken markets across the globe, and China is no exception. Some of the country’s top tech, finance, consumer, and healthcare companies have suffered significant hits as trade tensions between the U.S. and China have boiled over, despite strong financial foundations and sector-leading performance.

The widespread volatility is enough to unnerve even the most seasoned investors. But, those who resist panic-selling and remain steadfast in their investment strategies are typically rewarded with higher long-term gains. Even better, a downmarket can present strategic opportunities to invest in beaten-down stocks poised for strong returns once the storm passes.

At the end of last month, I laid out my top 5 ‘Biggest Losers’ worth buying the dip on, all of which have fantastic potential for a resurgence despite recent declines. In this article, I’ll discuss five top Chinese stocks that the market has similarly pummeled despite their strong underlying fundamentals. Each selection boasts a Quant Strong Buy rating, powerful momentum and growth outlook, and a discounted valuation—all of which leave it well-positioned for a potential comeback once volatility subsides.

You may be familiar with the adage, “Fear creates losses.” The precept highlights how panic selling during market downturns can lead to financial setbacks. Since 2010, the market has pulled back five times by more than 15% (July 1, 2010; August 8, 2011; December 20, 2018; March 9, 2020; and May 9, 2022). Investors who purchased the top 10 Quant Strong Buy stocks during these 15% downturns and held them for 2 years saw 117% gains on average vs. the S&P 500’s 50% return. SA Quant Strong Buys are based on backtesting and not actual recommendations at the time and subsequent performance results.

SA Quant Strong Buys Surged An Avg. 117% Following 15% Market Corrections

Despite being some of the Chinese market’s ‘Biggest Losers,’ my five selections detailed below are also benefiting from powerful industrial tailwinds—leading innovations in AI, healthcare, fintech, and more—despite being dragged down by widespread investor panic. Over the past month, these stocks posted an average return of -18% vs. the S&P 500’s -5.75%, but remain 'Strong Buys' due to their strong fundamentals and sector-leading performance.

China’s ‘Biggest Losers’

Each stock was selected using Seeking Alpha’s Quant System. This method combines powerful computer processing with our special ‘Quantamental’ analysis, designed to remove emotion from the investment process. The SA algorithm separates the weak companies from the strong ones based on their historical financials, analysts' consensus growth estimates, and hundreds of financial metrics. Using these insights, it ranks them by valuation, growth, profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions, assigning letter grades to each category to help contextualize thousands of data points.

Let’s dive into my top five ‘Biggest Losers’ with big potential.

1. LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. (NASDAQ: LX)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.2B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 685

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 39

Ranked first among all financials and consumer finance stocks, LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. is a Chinese consumer finance platform and e-commerce business. The company offers customers personal loans to purchase items on its e-commerce site Fenquile, which carries everything from electronics to clothing to household essentials. Users can also make purchases outside Fenquile by linking LX’s loans to other payment providers, including Alibaba's Alipay. The Shenzhen-based group posted strong Q4 2024 results, with a 17% quarterly increase in net income—its fourth consecutive quarter of improved profitability—and a 2% quarterly increase in loan origination to RMB $52B, or $7.02B. It declared a $0.11 dividend shortly after.

LX has shed around 30.5% over the past month amid the recent global sell-off and the escalation of the U.S.-China trade war since Trump’s ‘Liberation Day.’ However, its strong fundamentals—backed by ‘A’ Quant Factor Grades across the board—underline its potential for long-term growth despite geopolitical pressures.

LX Stock’s Quant Breakdown

LX’s ‘A-’ valuation is backed by a fantastic forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 4.13, a 57.4% discount from the 9.7 sector median. A forward price-to-sales ratio of 0.57, a 76% discount, emphasizes the low price of its shares relative to its potential sales growth as it continues to improve its suite of lending opportunities.

Growth is equally strong with forward EBITDA growth of 31.7%, a 183% difference from the sector, and long-term earnings per share (EPS) growth of 43.28%—a 358% difference vs. the 9.4% sector median. Profitability has improved from a ‘B-’ to an ‘A-’ over the past three months, as evidenced by its latest earnings results. Its momentum has remained strong during the recent downturn, with a one-year price performance of 310%—a 3,217% difference to the 9.3% sector median.

Like LX, my next pick continues to dominate the Quant rankings in its sector despite the ongoing trade war.

2. Kingsoft Cloud Holdings Limited (NASDAQ: KC)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.2B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 22

The reigning Quant champ of the technology sector as well as in the internet services and infrastructure industry, Kingsoft Cloud Holdings Limited, is a Chinese cloud services provider offering infrastructure as a service ((IaaS)) infrastructure, platform as a service (PaaS) middleware, and software as a service (SaaS) applications. The Beijing-based company returned a whopping 385% over the past six months, even though it lost around 25% over the past 30 days amid rising trade tensions. The group has invested heavily in AI, broadening its customer base and engaging in a strategic partnership with Chinese electronic giant Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) as it rises to take on China’s internet giants as an independent player. As SA analyst The Value Pendulum noted earlier this year:

Goldman Sachs (GS) projected that KC's AI revenue contribution as a percentage of its top line could rise to 26% and 31% in 2025 and 2026…KC's overall top-line performance is likely to be even better in the future, taking into account the positive AI revenue growth momentum.

Those efforts have already begun to pay off. The group reported Q4 GAAP EPS of -$0.01, beating expectations by $0.10. Total revenue growth doubled year-over-year to 30%, boosted by 34% Y/Y revenue growth of its public cloud business and 23% Y/Y revenue growth of its enterprise cloud revenue.

KC Stock’s Quant Breakdown

Forward revenue growth of 15.8%, an impressive 137% difference from the sector median, reflects KC’s strong AI tailwinds and improving profitability despite the ongoing trade war. “This quarter, we achieved both revenue growth and profitability improvement, marking a turnaround from the company's longstanding history of operating losses and marching towards the goal of net profit breakeven,” Vice Chairman and CEO Tao Zou told investors last month. KC’s valuation remains on the higher side, likely a result of the Chinese government’s increasing emphasis on AI following the successful rise of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), which catapulted its share price. Profitability has slowly improved over the past six months, rising from a ‘D-’ to a ‘D.’ Its scaled-up AI investments are reflected by an ‘A+’ CAPEX/sales ratio (TTM) of 53%, a 2,364.2% difference from the median.

My next selection hails from the world of e-commerce, but has demonstrated similar resilience to tariffs and trade tensions by doubling down on domestic growth.

3. JD.com, Inc. (NASDAQ: JD)

  • Market Capitalization: $54.3B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 5 out of 479

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 27

Headquartered in Beijing, China, JD.com, Inc. is the must-needed supply chain-based technology and logistics company offering various products and marketplace services. It develops, owns, and manages logistics facilities. The Beijing-based group is reportedly setting up a $27.B fund to boost the domestic sales of China’s top exporters as the U.S.-China trade war heats up, helping it make SA’s list of top China stocks. Despite losing 15% over the past month, it has returned over 8% year-to-date and nearly 48% over the past year—reflecting its remaining upside potential.

The company has a healthy balance sheet with over $7.5B in cash, significant year-over-year cash flow growth, and an attractive valuation that overshadows trade-related headwinds. As SA analyst Kevin George recently wrote:

The company's current valuation is far too low compared to the underlying trends, and I believe it is a good time to invest. Trump's tariffs could lead to a boost in domestic consumption in China, and we may also be seeing a ‘peak in U.S. exceptionalism,’ according to BofA analysts. That puts China in a better position to grab investment flows if the economy can get a boost.

JD Stock’s Quant Breakdown

JD touts an attractive valuation compared to many of its tech peers, in the U.S. and China, which has grown cheaper in response to its falling shares. Its forward price-to-earnings growth (PEG) ratio comes at a more than 20% discount to the sector median, while its forward non-GAAP P/E of 8.08 represents a sector-leading 41% discount.

Growth remains solid, with forward EPS diluted growth of 18.3%, a 196% difference to the 6.2% sector median, and long-term EPS growth of 28%, a 268% difference. Momentum continues to dominate the wider consumer discretionary sector as the trade war has weighed on e-commerce and other major Chinese exporters, with a three-month price performance of nearly 10% compared to the -16.3% median.

Like JD, my next pick is finding new and innovative ways to press forward amid mounting tariffs.

4. Qifu Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ: QFIN)

  • Market Capitalization: $5.4B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 6 out of 685

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 2 out of 39

Qifu Technology, Inc. is an AI-powered credit platform operating under the Qifu Jietiao brand in China. The company’s stock surged last month after it increased its semi-annual dividend by 17%, posted better-than-expected Q4 earnings, and issued strong 2025 guidance. That better-than-expected performance has helped QFIN remain in the green on a YTD basis despite losing around 15% over the past month. China recently unveiled a “special action plan” to boost domestic spending. The increased economic stimulus, paired with continued improvements in QFIN’s AI capabilities and user acquisition, could give it the boost it needs to emerge from the trade war unscathed.

As SA analyst Astrada Advisors recently wrote:

We believe that leveraging AI will be key in driving growth on both the supply and demand sides. QFIN has already pivoted towards this path to improve operational efficiency, which, we believe, will translate to financial results this year.

QFIN Stock’s Quant Breakdown

QFIN’s valuation stands out amongst its financial peers. Its forward non-GAAP P/E of 5.42 represents a more than 44% discount to the 9.69 sector median. Its ‘A+’ forward PEG of 0.39 is even more attractive, at a 66% discount to the 1.4 sector median.

QFIN’s increased spending on AI and domestic growth tailwinds from the Chinese government are reflected by forward EPS GAAP growth of 30.3%, a 221% difference to the sector median, and forward EBITDA growth of 21.6%, a 92.6% difference. Profitability has improved from a ‘B+’ to an ‘A-’ over the past three months, as reflected by a return on total assets (TTM) of 13%, a 1,025.9% difference from the sector. With seven positive EPS revisions over the past 90 days and zero negative, analysts agree that QFIN’s growth will likely keep revving up despite trade obstacles.

5. Zai Lab Limited (NASDAQ: ZLAB)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.4B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 19 out of 1007

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 9 out of 497

Zai Lab Limited is a biopharmaceutical company focused on discovering, developing, and marketing treatments across oncology, immunology, neuroscience, and infectious diseases. Its top drugs include Zejula, a cure for ovarian cancer, and NUZYRA, a treatment for skin infections. Despite losing around 20% over the past month, the Shanghai-based group’s powerful revenue growth and groundbreaking medical treatments have helped keep momentum strong amid growing trade tensions.

The company aims to achieve $2bn in annual revenues by 2028, a goal backed by strong financial performance with double-digit topline growth in 2024.

The drugmaker manufactures many of its treatments in the U.S. and Europe for local distribution, which could help shield it from tariffs. As SA analyst Myriam Alvarez recently noted on some of its top products:

ZLAB should be safe from these tariffs due to their manufacturing structure. Also, Vyvgart’s ongoing commercialization efforts are likely going to keep paying off, so it should further derisk it even if trade war tensions continue ramping up. You see, ZLAB actually manufactures and commercializes this particular drug locally in China, so in that sense, it should be relatively insulated from tariffs.

ZLAB Stock’s Quant Breakdown

ZLAB’s valuation has remained at a 'D+' as its stock has soared 114% over the past year, but we could soon see its share price come down due to trade pressures—even as growth and momentum stay elevated. Powerful growth tailwinds are reflected by forward revenue growth of 46.6%, a 498% difference to the 7.8% sector median. Excellent momentum is reflected by a three-month price performance of nearly 30%, a significant difference from the -19.4% median shared by the healthcare sector.

With six positive EPS revisions over the past 90 days and one negative, analysts broadly agree that temporary trade war woes will not permanently weigh on ZLAB’s performance. ZLAB, like the other four Chinese stocks discussed in this piece, continues to demonstrate resilience in the face of a fast-escalating trade war—a testament to its strong financials and ‘Strong Buy’ ratings.

Conclusion

Like many U.S. counterparts, China’s top-performing companies have been hammered by tariffs and trade war tensions over the past month, despite their strong balance sheets and sector-leading Quant Factor Grades. But the market always returns to fundamentals. All five selections in this piece have the necessary ingredients to ride out the tariff war despite temporary declines.

During periods of uncertainty and market volatility, I’ve often written and frequently emphasized the value of a barbell investing approach, combining fundamentally strong, opportunistically mispriced stocks with value-driven, dividend-paying equities to balance risk and enhance stability in volatile markets. Striking that balance requires removing emotion from the process and thinking critically through each decision. Before succumbing to nerves, utilize SA’s Quant System to examine your holdings' underlying metrics and Quant Factor Grades. Sometimes, you may find that the biggest losers still carry the biggest potential.

data.title: Zurn Elkay Water Solutions: Nice Growth, But Not At A Nice Price
data.description:
My search for interesting companies has brought me once again to another intriguing business. This is none other than Zurn Elkay Water Solutions Corporation (NYSE:ZWS), a company that management describes as a growth oriented, pure play water management firm that offers up a portfolio of specification-driven water management solutions. Over the past few years, this enterprise has seen revenue grow nicely. Profits and cash flows have followed a similar trajectory. Moving forward, management expects continued growth. However, a company like this does not come cheap. I certainly would not say that shares are expensive. Having said that, I would say that they are fairly valued at this time.

Of course, it is important to be flexible as new data comes into the picture. And it just so happens that we will be seeing some new data shortly. That is because, after the market closes on April 22nd, management will be reporting financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point in time, analysts expect continued revenue and profit growth. Unless the company can really wow me, however, I don't think that would be enough to justify a more bullish assessment than the ‘hold’ I have decided to rate it for now.

Taking a sip of Zurn Elkay Water Solutions

If my initial description of Zurn Elkay Water Solutions is nebulous, it's only because management's initial description of the business is that way. To really understand the firm, it would be helpful if we dig deeper into it. For starters, the company has a portfolio of professional grade water safety and control products, flow systems products, hygienic and environmental products, and filtered drinking water products that are used for both the public and private aspects of society. The goal here is to improve the health and hydration of its customers while also promoting human safety and environmental care.

Through major brands such as Zurn, Elkay, Wilkins, Green Turtle, World Dryer, and more, the company caters to a wide array of customers. On the institutional construction side, for instance, it works with players in education, the healthcare industry, and government agencies. The commercial construction end users for the enterprise consist of retailers, accommodations firms, restaurants, warehouses, sports arenas, office property owners, and more. It also caters to the waterworks and market by selling its products to municipal water and wastewater entities, and also those that deal in the transportation space.

Over the past few years, management has used its roughly 1,100 independent sales representatives spread across 180 different agencies to sell its products in around 30 countries. The results have been phenomenal during this time. From 2022 through 2024, revenue has shot up from $1.28 billion to $1.57 billion. Most of this growth occurred from 2022 to 2023 when revenue jumped an impressive 19.4%. This rise was because of a major merger that the company completed that saw Zurn Water Solutions Corporation combine with Elkay Manufacturing Company. But growth is growth all the same. The more modest increase in sales from 2023 to 2024, about 2.4% in total, was thanks to core sales growth of about 3%, which management claimed was caused by growth across almost all of its product categories. But beyond that, management didn't really provide much in the way of detail here.

On the bottom line, the picture has been quite nice. Over the last three years, net income has skyrocketed from $61.7 million to $160.2 million. Even on an adjusted basis, which excludes things like restructuring expenses and the amortization of intangible assets, net profits would have risen from $144.3 million to $223.9 million. Operating cash flow approximately tripled from $97 million to $293.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an approximate doubling from $144.9 million to $283.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $264.6 million to $390.4 million.

Management expects continued growth to occur this year. Even though the commercial market should see a reduction in revenue at a rate that will be in the low single digit range, and even though the residential market will be essentially flat, there should be growth associated with the institutional market and the waterworks market. This core sales growth that should be in the low single digit range should allow EBITDA this year to be between $405 million and $420 million. Given this and the other guidance provided by management, we should anticipate net profits of around $265.6 million and adjusted operating cash flow of about $300 million.

If we use these estimates and the historical results from last year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. While the price to earnings multiple for 2024 is a bit on the lofty side, all of the other trading multiples for the company are in a range that I would consider to be fair value. Relative to similar companies, shares also seem to be in the fair value range. In the table below, I compared it with five such enterprises. On both a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, I found that three of the five companies were cheaper than it is. This number does drop to one of the five when using the price to operating cash flow approach.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Zurn Elkay Water Solutions 22.7 17.9 13.8
CSW Industrials (CSWI) 33.7 27.6 21.5
Armstrong World Industries (AWI) 22.0 21.9 12.9
Trex Company (TREX) 26.4 41.4 17.6
Simpson Manufacturing (SSD) 19.4 18.5 12.5
UFP Industries (UFPI) 15.6 9.7 8.1

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will be reporting financial results for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on April 22nd. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will be around $383.6 million. Should this come to fruition, it would be 2.6% above the $373.8 million the company reported the previous year. They also expect earnings per share to be around $0.22. That would be up from the $0.19 per share reported for the first quarter of 2024 and would translate to a rise in net profits from $34.3 million to $38 million.

On an adjusted basis, the current expectation is for earnings of $0.29 per share. That would match what the company achieved last year and would mean adjusted net profits of $50.2 million. In the table above, you can also see other profitability metrics for the first quarter of 2024. Even though we don't have estimates provided by analysts for these, I would wager that a rise in revenue and profits would likely translate to a rise in these metrics as well.

One other thing that I would like to touch on is that Zurn Elkay Water Solutions has some other positive attributes to it. For starters, as you can see by looking at the image above, the net leverage ratio for the company has come down over the last couple of years. This was aided by the merger that allowed a rise in revenue, profits, and cash flows. The company also does not have any debt maturities until 2028. During this time, the enterprise is focused not only on growing operations, but also on returning capital to shareholders. The business recently increased its quarterly dividend by 13%. And in 2024, management oversaw the repurchase of 4.7 million shares for about $150 million. That works out to roughly 3% of the shares that were outstanding at that time. What's more, the company still has about $240 million remaining under its share buyback program. In all likelihood, additional share purchases are in the cards. Considering how the stock is priced, I would much prefer management allocate this extra capital toward growth. But at least shares aren't overvalued.

Takeaway

From the data that I can see at this time, I do think that Zurn Elkay Water Solutions is an interesting business. But in all honesty, it's not for me. Despite attractive growth in recent years and a track record for reducing leverage while simultaneously returning capital to shareholders, the company isn't cheap enough to justify a bullish assessment. For now, the best thing that I can do is rate it a ‘hold’. I doubt that the upcoming earnings data will change that opinion. But it could.

data.title: Tariff Turbulence Clouds Outlook For European Banks' M&A Moves
data.description:
The market and economic disruption triggered by the announcement of tariffs on all US imports will be a major distraction for the handful of European banks engaged in mergers and acquisitions and could complicate the execution of deals underway.

Spanish bank Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA's (BBVA) €12.3 billion move for domestic rival Banco de Sabadell SA (OTCPK:BNDSF) and three deals in Italy — Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA's (OTCPK:BMDPF) €13.3 billion bid for Mediobanca Banca di Credito Finanziario SpA (OTCPK:MDBIF)(OTCPK:MDIBY), UniCredit SpA's (OTCPK:UNCFF) €14 billion offer for Banco BPM, and BPER Banca's (OTCPK:BPXXY) €4.3 billion acquisition of Banca Popolare di Sondrio — could be impacted.

"If I were running a bank, I would have other fish to fry than think about merging with other banks right now," Sam Theodore, an independent bank analyst, said in an interview. "I would make sure that I keep the business I already have under control."

The tariffs announced on April 2 by US President Donald Trump caused a massive sell-off in European bank stocks and upended economic projections for the eurozone and other markets in which European banks operate. European bank stocks fell nearly 16% in the days after Trump's tariff announcement before recovering some of those losses after his April 9 partial U-turn, according to S&P Europe BMI Banks Index data.

The sharp sell-off in European bank stocks reflected investor concerns about the potentially negative impact tariffs could have on lenders' profitability and asset quality.

Increased caution

The worries sparked by the tariffs are likely to impact how banks view recently announced transactions and other potential opportunities for inorganic growth, Marco Troiano, head of financial institutions at Scope Ratings, said in an email.

"With the outlook for asset quality worsening and uncertainty broadening, bank management teams may become more cautious [about M&A deals]," said Troiano.

Some of the banks targeted in the deals underway have cited the uncertainty generated by the tariffs as further justification for their resistance to a takeover. Mediobanca CEO Alberto Nagel said the worsening macroeconomic conditions created by the tariffs made the disadvantages of a merger with Monte dei Paschi more evident, according to a Reuters report.

Italian bank stocks were among the hardest hit during the recent sell-off. Italy was the European Union's third-largest exporter to the US by volume in 2024 at €64.76 billion. Prime Minister Georgia Meloni and the country's top business lobby warned earlier in the month that US tariffs would severely impact Italy's exports and already struggling economy.

'Enhanced scrutiny'

Fresh analysis of the basis of ongoing offers is likely to be underway by bidders, targets and shareholders, Benoit Gérard, EMEIA financial services strategy and transactions leader at EY, said in an email.

"As the US tariff announcements continue to impact market and investor sentiment, increased caution when it comes to M&A is expected, at least in the short term, with enhanced scrutiny of deal rationale and value creation potential," said Gérard.

Proceed as planned

Still, the ongoing deals in Europe should have a sound enough basis to survive the current volatility in markets and doubts about the trajectory of eurozone growth, Andrea Costanzo, vice president, financial institutions at rating agency Morningstar DBRS, said in an interview.

"The performance of the financial markets after the announcement makes the environment extremely uncertain," said Costanzo. "But our view is that, provided that these offers have been announced with a sort of strategic and industrial rationale, the potential buyers would likely continue and proceed with the transaction."

The all-share structure of the proposed deals in Italy and Spain should also mitigate the impact of the sell-off in European bank stocks on the proposed deals, Costanzo added.

"If you look at the performance of European banks and especially Italian banks in this M&A, you don't see material differences between the movement of the banks' share prices since the announcement of the tariffs," said Costanzo. "Given that this has affected all the banks' shares, we would expect the exchange ratio of the offer share versus share to have a not very material impact."

Some changes to the structure of the proposed deals are possible if bank stocks incur further losses. "If share prices continue to drop, but banks remain confident in their fundamentals, it could make cash deals more attractive," said Troiano.

Significant drops could even open up the possibility of other M&A deals, Nuria Álvarez, bank equity analyst at Madrid-based investment services firm Renta 4 Banco, said in an email.

"I don't see the collapse in the share prices as a problem for M&A," said Álvarez. "The decline in the share prices could be an opportunity more than a drag."

Q1 highs

European bank share prices enjoyed a bumper start to 2025 before the tariff announcement. The S&P Europe BMI Banks Index rose by more than a third at its peak in the first quarter as investors responded to positive forecasts for the eurozone economy amid a proposed loosening of German fiscal policy and improved returns for lenders.

The eurozone faces a 10% tariff on most exports to the US, following Trump's decision to pause his original 20% reciprocal tariffs on the bloc for 90 days. Sectors including automotives, steel and aluminum face higher tariffs of 25%. The US has also placed a 10% tariff on UK imports.

A 10% tariff on most EU exports to the US would reduce eurozone GDP by about 0.2%, according to UK-based consultancy Capital Economics. But the damage could be greater due to the US administration's "flip-flopping," which is adding to the already high level of uncertainty, it added.

A lack of visibility for European banks and investors makes any additional M&A moves by European banks beyond those currently underway highly unlikely, said Costanzo.

"I don't expect to see an increase in these kinds of transactions until we get some additional clarity on the tariffs and the reaction from the European Union," he said. "As long as this uncertainty remains, it's usually not very good for investments."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TotalEnergies: Structural Earnings Rebound
data.description:
Following our last update, Drilling For Alpha, we are surprised to see that TotalEnergies SE (NYSE:TTE)(OTCPK:TTFNF)'s stock price has been flattish on a total return basis compared to a double-digit market decline. Since January-end, given the rapidly evolving market landscape, it is essential to consider the latest developments. In detail, US tariffs and the trade war with China will likely weigh on economic growth this year with the expectation of a lower oil demand. Additionally, eight OPEC+ members, including Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Russia, decided to raise oil production by 411,000 barrels per day in May 2025. This increase is triple the initially planned and is part of a strategy to unwind previous output cuts gradually.

As such, we are reducing our Brent forecasts by $7 per barrel from $75 to $68 per barrel. We also believe the market will likely be oversupplied with oil.

Concerns over a potential global recession have heightened volatility across financial and oil markets. Currently, the strong positive correlation between U.S. equities and crude oil prices suggests that demand-side dynamics are playing a key role in price movements. While the Energy sector has historically outperformed during recessionary periods, the situation is now more complex due to the additional supply introduced by OPEC+.

Fig 1

According to the Dallas Fed Energy Survey, we might anticipate a further slowdown in U.S. shale drilling activity if oil prices go down to $65 per barrel — this is the average breakeven level for new drilling projects in the U.S. This is positive for TotalEnergies. Meanwhile, our base expectation is that OPEC+ will maintain flat production levels from May onward, barring significant supply disruptions. On a more constructive note for TotalEnergies, geopolitical developments could offer some upside support. This might include 1) new tariffs on Venezuela, 2) revocation of U.S. operating licenses for several companies by the Trump administration, and 3) tighter sanctions on Iran and Russia. Aside from a modest decline in Venezuelan output, further escalation could provide firmer Brent price support.

Why are we still Positive?

There is a combination of objectives that make us positive. The company is set to grow from a structural rebound in earnings. This is mainly driven by higher gas prices, LNG and oil production growth, and fixing downstream operational issues. So, we report the following:

  1. Last time, we reported a positive stance on the company's LNG earnings thanks to higher gas prices. On LNG, the company guided for an average selling price of $10/Mbtu. This implied approximately $500 million year-on-year higher profits as part of TotalEnergies's $7 billion CFFO target. Inventory declines (Fig 2) and a higher-than-expected winter consumption support this. Additionally, the French energy giant is increasing capacity by +5%, which is coming online in 2025, with a current tight market until 2027;
  2. On the oil downstream, the management highlighted the year-on-year operational improvement, particularly at the Donges and Normandy refineries. The company is progressing energy efficiency saving programs for a total saving of over $100 million per year. More importantly, TotalEnergies is targeting >85% refinery utilisation compared to results of 79% in the Fiscal Year 2024. Following the Q4 results, the company reiterated its intention to capture opportunities from an ongoing restructuring industry from refinery-to-biorefinery transformations;
  3. On the company's CAPEX, the organic investment outlook was reduced from $18 billion to $17 billion in 2025 (Fig 3). There is an additional $1 billion in organic CAPEX flexibility despite market conditions. According to our calculation, Capex reduction was mainly backed by lower investment in EV charging and biogas projects. The other half was reduced to upstream projects;
  4. On a positive note, the company continues to guide solid shareholders' remuneration combined with a gearing ratio of 12-13%, which is also consistent with our internal expectations. This offers downside protection in a volatile market. Indeed, the company's dividend yield is at 6.5% at the current market price, and including a Brent price of $70 barrel per day means a quarterly buyback of up to $2 billion (Fig 4). We are currently reducing our buyback assumption starting from Q2 2025, pricing a $3 billion YTD buyback;
  5. On a peer analysis, Shell and TotalEnergies are the most recession-resistant shares, with less sensitivity to refining margins and low share price beta to Brent. Eni is also a defensive play, albeit with a slightly more geared balance sheet than peers. On the other hand, we are more negative about Repsol and BP.

Source: TotalEnergies Q4 results presentation - Fig 2

Fig 3

Fig 4

Earnings Change and Valuation

Following the Q4 earnings release and considering the latest oil price changes, we reviewed our TotalEnergies 2025 financials estimates. The company's CFFO outlook was reassuring and supports our view. Still, we project lower earnings revision following the changes in Brent price. Our long-term assumption on oil and EU TTF gas price ($/MMBtu) remains unchanged. That said, moving the Brent price from $75 to $68, we lowered our upstream division's core operating profit from $16.5 to $14.54 billion in 2025. On a positive note, we increased our EBIT forecast by $500 million on the LNG side. This moved our EBIT profit forecast from a previous result of $27.9 billion to $26.44 billion. The Integrated LNG CFFO target is now $6 billion and incorporates a $500 million improvement in gas trading. The CEO stressed that the lower European gas storage levels support the LNG price and likely increase volatility. Our EBIT declined by approximately 5%, and there is a corporate tax rate of 39% to consider (this is aligned with Total's historical average). With lower CAPEX and a reduction in the buyback, our net debt is aligned with the company's expectation of almost $15 billion (11% gearing ratio). With a $1.5 billion in interest payment, our net income projections are set at $17 billion with an EPS of $7.48 (from a previous EPS of $8.18, which included a lower share count in our last update).

Continuing to apply a blended methodology with a P/E target of 8x and an EV/EBITDA of 4.5x, considering a lower EPS projection, our valuation moved from $65.4 to $62 per share. This implied an 8.7% valuation upside and does not consider a dividend yield of 6.5%. On the EV/EBITDA, the company trades at 3.8x. In Fig 5, we can see that both ratios are below the company's 5-year latest historical average.

Fig 5

Risks

We report the following downside risks: 1) any changes in LNG and oil prices with impacts on profits, 2) lower-than-forecasted production growth (following higher production estimates of +5% in LNG and oil production growth of +3%), 3) FX changes, 4) lower refining margins, 5) project execution risks and CAPEX delay, 6) value destruction M&A, 7) terrorist attacks, 8) higher tax rates, given TotalEnergies geographical footprint. Indeed, the company operates in some regions susceptible to civil unrest. Additionally, even if we report a lower buyback, investors might be sensitive to changes in the distribution policy and higher gearing increases. We believe the company made the right decision to lower its organic CAPEX.

Conclusion

Despite a challenging market momentum, the company has a low sensitivity to oil prices. Considering lower E&P results and lower CAPEX investments, the company will likely manage a higher debt level while still remunerating the shareholders. The company is still a buy with a valuation discount compared to the past and structural savings ahead.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Just Take A Bite Of This Cookie
data.description:
Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) was a welcome addition to my portfolio last week. The recent decline in the stock has led me to conclude that the price is very attractive.

The stock has grown into a strong player in the advertising market since its IPO in 2016. However, the stock took a big hit earlier this year due to a disappointing quarterly report. The stock is now trading 64% lower than its all-time high. And for investors who got in September 2020, there is no good news either, as they have not earned any returns due to the crash.

The quarterly report wasn’t all that bad, though. Revenues rose, margins improved, and the market is full of growth potential. I have no reason to believe the company is fundamentally weak. I see this as an excellent entry point for long-term investors who can buy shares of a quality company at a nice discount now.

The Trade Desk: The Independent Advertiser

The advertising market has changed significantly in recent years. In the past, advertising contracts were processed manually, but today they are processed via algorithms, data-driven and real-time bidding. This is called Programmatic Advertising and includes all digital channels, such as advertising on radio or audio, YouTube, but also on TV.

A fast-growing advertising market is Connected TV (CTV). Think of advertising services on Netflix, Disney+ and Hulu. Consumers seem to be switching from traditional TV subscriptions to these streaming services. And they can choose a subscription or a reduced subscription with advertisements. And that is where The Trade Desk comes in. They arrange the purchase of advertising space via thousands of publishers, apps and video platforms outside the walled garden of Google or Meta (a walled garden is a closed ecosystem in which Google or Meta has full control over the ads).

The Trade Desk positions itself in the advertising world as a neutral intermediary; it is an open and transparent platform that gives customers control. Google and Amazon have their own closed systems. The Trade Desk works with Walmart, among others, where retailers use their customer data to target ads. It is called "Retail Media" and offers enormous possibilities.

TTD Operates In A Niche Market With Innovative Ideas

The Trade Desk distinguishes itself from Google, Amazon and Meta in one important way. TTD’s strategy is to be independent. Google, Amazon and Meta are both buyers and sellers in the advertising industry, while TTD only wants to serve advertisers. That creates trust.

Several changes are taking place in the advertising market. The introduction of Unified ID 2.0 (replacement for third-party cookies) shows that changes are coming to the advertising market. Cookies will soon be phased out and advertisers are looking for privacy-friendly alternatives. TTD introduced the AI-driven advertising platform Kokai in 2023. This engine helps advertisers to plan, analyze and optimize campaigns smarter. It is an essential part of TTD.

Also notable is the development of its own operating system for smart TVs, called Ventura. With this, TTD does not want to be dependent on third parties for access to the CTV inventory. This makes targeting much easier because viewing data and user experiences can be easily retrieved.

However, I am skeptical about the adoption of Ventura by big brands like Samsung, Sony or Philips. These manufacturers have their own ecosystems and commercial agreements for advertising revenue. TTD will (I think) first target small manufacturers. In my opinion, Ventura is a step towards more independence, but not a threat to the established TV ecosystems.

A Strong Name

There is fierce competition in the world of programmatic advertising. Google’s DV360 is the most well-known DSP (Demand Side Platform). Amazon offers similar services with access to its own e-commerce traffic. And Meta (Facebook and Instagram) primarily sells through both platforms. They all have the powerful scale but lack the neutrality that The Trade Desk offers.

The real competition comes from other independent platforms like AppLovin. With its demand-side Axon platform, it is strong in executing AI-driven ads. Last year, the company grew strongly, especially in performance advertising for gaming and mobile apps. AppLovin excels in mobile campaigns focused on app installs and ROAS goals. The Trade Desk focuses on brand advertising, premium content and cross-channel reach including CTV, display, audio and retail media. This makes AppLovin more dependent on the gaming and entertainment industry, which in my opinion makes it a bit more volatile than The Trade Desk. TTD serves a much broader customer base, spread across different sectors and ad formats. Therefore, I do not see them as direct competitors at the moment.

What I like about TTD is that they have achieved a decent customer retention of around 95% for more than 11 years in a row. The platform is also widely applicable and is able to capture market share in growth segments such as CTV. The Trade Desk has built a proven strategy and a good name with this.

A Mixed Bag

Now that we know TTD has a unique position in a growth market with few competitors, I’m curious to see what the quarterly results tell us.

The fourth quarter of 2024 was a mixed bag. Revenue rose 22% to $741 million, below analyst expectations. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $350 million, with a margin of 47%. And for the full year 2024, revenue rose 26% to $2.4 billion. TTD also generated more than $600 million in free cash flow.

However, TTD came in with a moderate outlook, expecting only 17% revenue growth in the first quarter of 2025. What also scared investors was the reorganization of the team and the optimization of processes. I think the stock has been punished too harshly.

TTD is financially robust, with over $1.9 billion in cash and no long-term debt. This makes it resilient to interest rate hikes and allows it to continue investing. The share buyback program has also been expanded to a $1 billion authorization. This represents a buyback yield of over 4%. The Trade Desk has a solid balance sheet, is very profitable and is growing both revenue and earnings. An interesting combination.

And while the valuation seems high, it should not be forgotten that both revenue and earnings are growing significantly. If we look at the EV/EBITDA ratio, we see that it is back to 2018 levels. I still think the EV/EBITDA ratio itself of 43x is high, but the strong growth prospects and the opportunity to increase EBITDA through efficiency improvements and AI are enough reason for me to take a first step.

Sector peer AppLovin is trading at a lower multiple of 35x but has lower growth potential as they generate twice the revenue of TTD. In my opinion, TTD’s share price is a great entry point and if the price drops further I will buy more.

Conclusion

The Trade Desk is struggling with a growth slowdown and opts for a reorganization. Investors reacted shocked, after which the share is listed 64% lower than its all-time high. TTD is financially very healthy, highly profitable and well positioned for the cookieless AI-driven advertising world.

The current share valuation, which is considerably lower than last year, offers long-term investors an attractive entry point. What I also like is that more than 95% of customers remain loyal to TTD, partly due to the technological developments in which TTD is always at the forefront. In addition, the strong cash flows and the debt-free balance sheet make TTD strong for the future. Rising interest rates have no direct impact on profits.

TTD offers advertisers exposure through various digital channels, where transparency and independence are central. And that makes it interesting for brands that want to experience independence. A niche market in which TTD is strongly positioned. The attractive fundamentals and valuation make this stock worth a buy recommendation.

data.title: Japanese Export Growth Slowed In March, And U.S. Tariffs Are Sure To Accelerate The Downshift
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Exports rose for a sixth straight month in March, but at a slower pace

Japanese exports rose 3.9% in March year on year (vs. 11.4% in February, 4.4% market consensus), partly due to the early effects of US tariffs.

By destination, exports to the US still gained 3.1% despite tariffs on iron, steel and aluminium. Indeed, exports of iron and steel (-16.6%) declined, but they only account for 1.3% of total exports. So far, the overall impact is muted. Major export items such as electrical machinery (6.1%) and transportation equipment (1.0%) gained, likely boosted by frontloading.

Meanwhile, exports to Asia grew 5.5%, which highlights some interesting dynamics. Exports to China, Japan’s second-largest export partner, dropped -4.8%, while shipments to Hong Kong (19.7%), Taiwan (19.5%), and South Korea (11.5%) all recorded double-digit increases. Also, semiconductors and chip-making machinery exports to China dropped significantly. This is likely due to the rerouting of exports within Asia to avoid tariff conflicts with the US. Since China is the largest producer of semiconductors, we’re concerned about a downward cycle in the sector.

Markets closely watching trade negotiations between the US and Japan

Trump claimed “big progress” on his Truth Social platform after discussions with Japan, but no details have been revealed yet. As expected, the US linked tariff talks to a Japanese spending increase for US troops in the country. Potentially, Japan is expected to commit to purchasing more US goods, including LNG and agricultural products, and increase direct investment in the US. The Japanese yen lost ground against the US dollar this morning on news the currency rate wasn't discussed, at least not yet. We expect the JPY to be one of the outperformers in the FX market. Volatility is likely, though, depending on trade discussions.

We believe a strong JPY is likely to alleviate some of the inflationary pressures for the Bank of Japan (BoJ). Yet, the BoJ is likely to keep its policy normalization strategy, as its 2% inflation target is likely to be met with strong wage growth and a recovery of household consumption. Also, the BoJ is cautious about sending dovish signals to the market. This could trigger JPY weakness and attract criticism from the US.

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: New Income Opportunities And Stress-Testing Our 2025 Playbook
data.description:
Tariffs have hit the market like a ton of bricks. Prices have fallen across assets bearing any sizable risk, credit spreads have widened and volatility has spiked. In this article, we benchmark this drawdown to provide a perspective on where exactly we are. We also revisit our 2025 playbook to see whether our allocation tilts have aged well. Finally, we highlight a number of opportunities that have been uncovered by this drawdown.

Benchmarking the Drawdown

In this section, we take a look at how this income drawdown has played out so far. There are few surprises here. As we discussed in a separate article on income drawdowns, particularly those with a macro shock flavor - higher-quality fixed-income did very well such as TIPS, Agencies, Munis. Given this drawdown was a macro rather than a rate shock, a drop in Treasury yields supported these sectors so that they actually rallied last week.

Other sectors that did reasonably well were Agency mortgage REIT preferreds, falling around 2.7% over the past month. For a sector with yields of 9-10% that is pretty remarkable. Baby bonds from sectors like BDCs and mortgage REITs did not move a whole lot, being roughly flat over the month. Finally, higher-quality open-end funds were resilient as well, including higher-rated CLO Debt, short-dated corporate debt and multi-sector investment-grade credit.

Sectors in the left tail of the distribution were higher-beta assets like CLO Equity CEFs, equity-linked sectors like covered calls, mortgage REITs, BDCs, Converts and others. These sectors are fairly reliable underperformers during periods of market weakness.

A couple of other things to highlight is that high-yield Munis did very well, managing to rally over the past month. This sector is often overlooked by investors despite its resilience and very attractive tax-adjusted yields. High-yield corporate bonds held in pretty well with a drop of around 2% due to the fall in Treasury yields, which supported duration assets.

Turning to the CEF market, sector performance mimics what has happened across the broader income market. It is also not surprising that discounts have exacerbated the moves in NAVs - a very common pattern that we see time and time again. The fact that discounts were relatively rich, as we have discussed any number of times in our CEF Weeklies did not help things.

CEF volume over the week was elevated and was only significantly higher during the COVID period and the 2018 Fed "autopilot" error. This points to an extreme level of stress in the market and a high level of investor risk aversion.

The percentage of decliners over the week was relatively high but off the previous peaks in the last 5 years.

The average CEF drawdown is a tad larger than 10% Over the past decade we have seen 6 other drawdowns that were larger than this. Four of these finished out not much lower at around 15-18% overall and two were much larger drawdowns of 30-40%.

High-yield corporate bond credit spreads are above their median value (though below the mean).

This is a very conservative statement given the quality of the sector has improved significantly over the last couple of decades, with BB - the highest rating bucket - going from 37% to 60% of the sector.

BDC valuations are well below their historical average, even if we take into account that NAVs are likely to come in 2-3% below their Q4 levels due to the widening in credit spreads.

BDC yields (on a 12m-TTM basis) have jumped by around 1.25% on the week and are now at their highest level outside of COVID and GFC (though with a likely downward trend if the Fed responds to this macro shock by cutting rates - a big if as inflation is expected to spike higher).

Turning to our one-stop chart for income market valuation, we see that both credit spreads and CEF discounts have widened sharply. We have been trading in the rich upper left quadrant for some time, and we took this opportunity to significantly dial down our CEF exposure over the last 18 months. It's good to see the market moving towards the more attractive lower right quadrant. The market was cheaper in 2022 (as well as 2020 and 2015 - not pictured) however this is a perfectly fine time to start adding to CEFs, and we intend to do so over the coming week.

A key question for investors is, does this drawdown feel like the much larger GFC, COVID or 2022 rate shock? Our view is not really. It is not an "existential" one like GFC and COVID, and it is not a rate shock but a supply/growth shock. This suggests that now is not an obviously bad time to be adding to higher-yielding / beaten down assets. This is particularly the case for investors who took some chips off the table over the last 18 months as markets grew increasingly rich.

In mid-2024 we discussed this idea when we updated the swirlogram which looked like the following at the time. Our view was that investors ought to be reducing their risk exposure as valuations looked increasingly rich. Investors who added to their dry powder allocation can now spend it on the market-wide sale.

2005 Playbook In Action

In our 2025 Playbook, we discussed a number of market features that guided our allocation. To sum it up, our views were the following:

  • Credit valuation was very rich and, hence, highly asymmetric to the downside
  • CEFs were not attractive in aggregate owing to tight discounts, expensive underlying assets and a low level of marginal yield on leveraged assets
  • Higher-yield / higher-beta assets were vulnerable to a downward repricing due to embedded leverage and lower-quality exposure.
  • Short-duration / shorter-maturity assets looked compelling due to the flat yield curve and lower beta profile
  • Higher-quality Municipal and inflation assets looked attractive due to their greater resilience and low opportunity costs of avoiding higher-yielding assets
  • Baby bonds were attractive drier-powder securities, offering investors an attractive yield while also providing enough resilience to use as a source of capital to rotate into higher-yielding assets during the next drawdown

Obviously, few if anyone at the start of the year had a clear view that we would see a sharp drawdown as a result of the new tariff policy. However, this highlights the value of managing an allocation in response to market valuation. Markets oscillate, drawdowns will happen and they are largely unpredictable.

Investors who are positioned in more resilient assets when drawdowns do happen are in a much better position to buy higher-beta / higher-yielding beaten down assets. An investor in a tax-exempt fund that is flat this year or in a handful of bonds that are down 1-3% is in a great position to add a CLO Equity / BDC asset that is down 10-20%. Investors who are "all in" at all times don't have this luxury.

A Note On The Barbell

About a week prior to the sell-off, we discussed a barbell allocation strategy and why it can be a good way to operationalize a countercyclical investment approach.

A countercyclical investment approach is one where investors tilt to higher-yielding / higher-risk assets when valuations cheapen and tilt to lower-yielding / lower-risk assets when valuations richen. Although this sounds sensible and is in line with the broader value / Buffett investment approach, many income investors tend to do the opposite (i.e. they follow a procyclical approach). When yields fall, they tend to stretch on the risk spectrum for higher-yielding assets because it feels safe to do so as "the times are good" and they want to maintain their previously higher yields. And then they sell higher-risk assets when markets start to weaken as it's "obvious" that we are going into a recession and that will punish higher-risk assets.

The key benefit of a countercyclical approach is that once investors accept that some assets (i.e. predominantly those that are higher-yield / higher-risk ones) tend to be more vulnerable during drawdowns, it's easy to understand that they can use more resilient assets to buy assets that fell harder in a drawdown. This can allow investors to achieve a sustainable pick-up in yield as well as grow their portfolio wealth over time. Investors who are always "all in" don't benefit as much from rebalancing opportunities, as there are fewer "dry powder" positions in the portfolio.

A barbell approach is a way to implement a countercyclical approach. It consists of, roughly speaking, a higher-quality portion and a higher-risk portion in the portfolio. The portions are sized individually according to overall investor risk appetite. For example, our Defensive Income Portfolio is predominantly in lower-risk assets while the High Income Portfolio was around 60% in lower-risk assets (it is less so now).

The definition of lower risk can vary from very low risk such as AAA CLOs and high-quality medium-duration Munis to Agency mortgage REIT preferreds and baby bonds. The former did not move at all in this latest drawdown or even rallied, while the latter sold off by around 1-3%.

As valuations richen, investors would rotate from the higher-risk portion of the barbell to the lower-risk portion and vice versa. Then as valuations cheapen as they did sharply last week, investors can tap the lower-risk portion of the barbell to rotate into the higher-risk / higher-yielding portion. Obviously, we don't know when this drawdown will come to an end, but it can make sense to rotate programmatically with every leg up in credit spreads / leg down in prices. This can be unnerving, as though trying to catch a falling knife, but, without knowing the ultimate endpoint of the drawdown, is the only way to participate in a more attractive market environment in a consistent and rational way.

Opportunities

The current market has opened up a number of attractive, high-yielding opportunities. In the CEF market we like the following securities:

  • A multi-sector credit CEF Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC), trading at an 11% current yield and a 9.4% discount
  • Loan / CLO Equity XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT), trading at a 19% current yield and a 9% discount
  • CLO Equity Eagle Point Credit Co (ECC), trading at a 23% current yield
  • The limited duration corporate bond CEF Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund (BGH), trading at a 9.2% current yield and an 8% discount

In BDCs, we like the following:

  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), trading at a 12.6% yield and a 13% discount
  • Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL), trading at a 10.7% yield and a 10% discount
  • Gladstone Investment (GAIN), trading at a 7.7% yield and a 7% discount. The total NAV return of the company over the last 3 years is close to double of its dividend yield.

We are rotating into some of these assets by using our lower-risk allocation to Muni funds. These funds are roughly flat year-to-date and offer a great source of resilient capital to take advantage of the sell-off in higher-yielding assets.

Takeaways

It is tempting to have a firm view of where we go from here. However, the speed of the market's reaction shows that few if any participants expected things to play out like they have. For now, the administration has dug in its heels on the tariffs going ahead. However, because the source of US tariffs are bilateral trade deficits rather than foreign tariffs, US tariffs will likely persist for some time as trade deficits are much more difficult to remove than tariffs. Our base case is that US tariffs will be whittled down over the rest of the year as countries lighten their non-tariff barriers. However, this also means that US tariffs will likely remain well above their 2024 levels. This will likely keep uncertainty and market volatility at elevated levels.

data.title: Elbit Systems: What Is Left After The 100% Surge?
data.description:
In August 2024, I upgraded Elbit Systems Ltd. (NASDAQ:ESLT) from hold to buy on tensions in the Middle East that resulted in a significant uptick in demand for defense equipment in Israel and accelerated fielding for the IDF (Israel Defense Forces).

A risk I saw was that a ceasefire in the Middle East could reduce near term demand from the IDF. However, ceasefires have shown to be fragile, and the tension has barely decreased despite mounting opposition against the war. Furthermore, it should be kept in mind that Elbit Systems is geographically diversified, with around 70% of the sales being for customers outside of Israel.

In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings and update my price target and rating as the stock has far exceeded my price targets after a more than 100% surge in the stock price.

Elbit Systems Sales And Margins Grow

In 2024, revenues grew 14.3% to $6.83 billion. Operating income grew by nearly a third to $489.1 million. Operating margins increased from 6.1% to 7% and that is a positive given that we previously saw the company increasing sales but on stable margins.

Aerospace revenues grew 9% to $2 billion, which was in line with the CAGR since 2021. The other segments showed growth exceeding the CAGR. C4I & Cyber, which focuses on command, control communications, communications, cyber and intelligence, saw sales growing 11% to $800 million while ISTAR & EW (Intelligence, Surveillance, Target Acquisition and Reconnaissance & Electronic Warfare) sales grew 12% to $1.3 billion.

Land System sales grew 29% to $1.68 billion, driven by ammunition sales growth. In 2025, the company will increase production through the annualized effect and ramp up of the new facility in Ramat Beka and international expansion.

ESA or Elbit Systems of America grew sales by 8% through higher night-vision systems sales.

Order Backlog Supports Three Years Of Revenues

Elbit Systems has a positive outlook to further grow its revenues. The backlog has grown to $22.6 billion. 65% of the backlog are international sales and half of the backlog will be delivered in 2025 and 2026. So, the company has good visibility on growth opportunities and backlog materializing.

Elbit Systems Stock Has Further Growth Ahead

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Elbit Systems exceeded expectations for 2024 by 3.9% on EBITDA, and free cash flow was substantially better than expected. Around a breakeven free cash flow was expected and $320 million in free cash flow was delivered.

For the years ahead, the EBITDA expectations have increased as well. EBITDA estimates for 2025 have been increased by 25% and by 32% for 2026. The annual growth is expected to be 20.6%. On free cash flow, expectations have increased as well, with cumulated free cash flow generation through 2027 of $1.92 billion.

With all this in mind, I am lifting my price target to $457.40, representing 13% upside. My prior target was $289.27 for 2026. So, the increase has been major and is driven by significantly higher expectations for EBITDA and free cash flow.

Conclusion: Elbit Systems Stock Can Head Higher

The earnings for 2024 showed strong double-digit growth in sales and margin expansion, delivering a major boost to the operating income. The backlog and global tension does support an acceleration in backlog conversion to sales. Elbit Systems is not a pure play on the turmoil in the Middle East, but it does provide a boost while the global tension provides significant support for arms exports.

With all of that in mind, I am maintaining my buy rating and increasing the price target to $457.40.

data.title: An Abrupt And Cascading Dislocation
data.description:
Brief observations

The Market Climate remains on a Crash Warning. It’s unfortunate, in my view, that investors have so much faith that a monetary easing can and will bail out the economy and the stock market. My view is fairly simple – the economic boom we’ve enjoyed has been driven by an inordinate amount of leverage, much of it of very poor credit quality, and by capital spending financed through the import of foreign savings. In an environment where demand for new capital investment was strong, easy bank credit and ample foreign savings fed extremely good economic growth rates. But I believe we are past that point here.” – John P. Hussman, Ph.D., Market Comment, December 19, 2000

It can be dangerous to attempt trading bear market rallies early into a decline – especially when valuations remain rich. It’s useful to remember that the 1929 and 1987 crashes started after the S&P 500 was already down about 14% from its highs. So emerging panic is not enough – there has to be some basis to believe that a positive shift in investor attitudes toward risk would be sustainable.” – John P. Hussman, Market Comment, June 23, 2008

Remember that the first line down following a market peak is typically vertical. The steep initial losses typically make investors feel like fools to sell into the decline, and the clearing rallies that follow convince them that everything is still fine. That process, in repeated sequence, is why investors tend to hold on the whole way down, often eventually capitulating at the lows.” – John P. Hussman, Ph.D., You’re Soaking In It, July 21, 2024

I’ve regularly described the period since early 2022 as the extended peak of the third great speculative bubble in U.S. history. As I noted in the April comment, Humpty Dumpty was Pushed, I believe that process is now complete. As discussed in that comment, our Recession Warning Composite also now indicates the likelihood of an oncoming recession in the U.S. economy.

In our day-to-day investment discipline, we’ve had several opportunities to vary the intensity of our defensive investment stance, some informed by the hedging implementation I described last September (for more, see Asking a Better Question, Subsets and Sensibility, and the section titled “The Martian” in The Turtle and the Pendulum), and some in response very short-lived “compression syndromes” that I’ve regularly discussed over the years.

While we remain open to changes in market conditions, as well as periodic “fast, furious, prone-to-failure” advances that can relieve the oversold “compression” produced by market losses, we are presently on high alert for a possibly abrupt and cascading market and economic dislocation in the weeks ahead.

The trap door swings open

At its core, a market crash is nothing but risk-aversion meeting a market that’s not priced to tolerate risk. We always become concerned about “trap door” outcomes when rich valuations are joined by deterioration in the uniformity of market internals – which is our most reliable gauge of speculation versus risk-aversion among investors.

Our concerns about trap door conditions become even more pointed when investor confidence has been destabilized. Recall that the 1929 and 1987 crashes started after the S&P 500 was already down about 14% from its highs. As a general rule-of-thumb, when investors are jolted by a decline in the S&P 500 of 10% or more from its 10-week high, and Baa corporate yields or credit spreads are widening, “trap door” conditions tend to be resolved by abrupt and often substantial market losses.

The chart below shows what this looks like, in data since 1928. The blue line is the S&P 500 Index (left scale, log). The red bars show instances that are assigned our most negative return/risk classification – based on measurable, observable market conditions such as valuations and market internals – coupled with a loss in the S&P 500 of more than 10% from its 10-week high and deteriorating Baa credit conditions (versus 6 months prior). The green line (right scale, log) is the cumulative return of the S&P 500 in the 65 weeks since 1928 when this particular set of conditions has been in effect. It’s a rare syndrome, but it has regularly occurred at the breakpoint of memorable market collapses, including 1929, 1987, 2000, 2008, and 2020.

Notice that the green line looks as if it’s almost a stairstep. That’s because steep losses are so common once the trap door swings open. I’ve periodically described this sort of environment as a “crash warning.” Emphatically, that term is not intended as a forecast, but simply as a shorthand for market conditions that are often followed by abrupt mismatch of desired selling versus desired buying, and are typically resolved with price declines large enough to bring the two into equilibrium. Again, not a single share is sold that is not also bought at the same moment. What matters for prices is which person – the seller or the buyer – is more eager.

The chart below shows the largest loss in the S&P 500 – on a weekly closing basis – in the 6-week period following each of the instances above.

One way to understand abrupt market losses is to consider what I’ve often called the “Iron Law of Equilibrium.” Every share of stock that’s been issued must be held by some investor at every point in time. Investors cannot “get out” of stocks, in aggregate, nor can they “get into” stocks, in aggregate. Every dollar a buyer brings “into” the market is taken “out of” the market by a seller. Every dollar a seller wishes to take “out of” the market has to come “into” the market from a buyer.

Consider, for simplicity, two sets of market participants – “trend-following” investors whose demand reflects backward looking market returns, and “value-conscious” investors whose demand reflects the level of market valuations and, by extension, expected future returns.

As I’ve shown in simulations of this kind of equilibrium, when prices are unusually elevated relative to the norm, it’s almost always because trend-followers (and other price-insensitive buyers) are “all in.” Those positions are – and in fact have to be – offset by equal and opposite underweights by value-conscious investors. A sudden decrease in the desired holdings of trend-followers and price-insensitive traders has to be satisfied by inducing a price decline large enough to give value-conscious investors an incentive to buy.

At its core, a market crash is nothing but risk-aversion meeting a market that’s not priced to tolerate risk. We always become concerned about “trap door” outcomes when rich valuations are joined by deterioration in the uniformity of market internals – which is our most reliable gauge of speculation versus risk-aversion among investors. Our concerns about trap door conditions become even more pointed when investor confidence has been destabilized. We are presently on high alert for a possibly abrupt and cascading market and economic dislocation in the weeks ahead.

As always, nothing in our investment discipline relies on forecasts, particularly involving extreme outcomes like those above. Instead, our discipline is to align our investment outlook with prevailing, measurable, observable market conditions, and to change our outlook as those conditions change. Even amid the initial break of what we view as the third great speculative bubble in U.S. history, we’ve had multiple opportunities to vary the intensity of our defensive outlook in response to market behavior, and we continue to watch for “compression syndromes” that can produce “fast, furious, prone-to-failure” market advances to clear oversold conditions.

For now, I would describe present market conditions as a “crash warning” – featuring an environment that includes extreme valuations, broken market internals, and destabilized investor confidence. Nothing in these conditions ensures a market collapse – abrupt crashes are the rarest of events, and investors should approach current risks with that understanding. Still, we should be aware that virtually all market crashes share a common feature – risk-aversion meeting a market that’s not priced to tolerate risk.

Because silence can be equal to consent, I’ll add that these unfavorable market conditions are joined by other observable elements that are deeply troubling in my view: the rapid descent of the U.S. toward an autocracy that normalizes the unchecked abuse of power; dispenses with the rule of law, due process, and human rights; dismantles democratic institutions; replaces career civil servants with loyalists; systematically defunds public health research and disease surveillance; retaliates against critics; raises barriers to access of Social Security offices and earned benefits; pressures law firms not to represent clients; exploits every opportunity for self-dealing; defunds universities, purges libraries, and whitewashes museums of so-called “improper ideology”; maligns journalists and bars access to news organizations that resist absurd demands; abandons, betrays, and disparages allies; openly seeks to annex sovereign countries; floods public dialogue with disinformation, grievance, fear, division, and false narratives; expels independent Inspectors General along with the capacity of the public to demand accountability; and deliberately erodes constitutional constraints in favor of personal dictate.

Find out just what any people will quietly submit to, and you have found out the exact measure of injustice and wrong which will be imposed upon them.” – Frederick Douglass, 1857

Perhaps the escalating risk of a market crash and cascading economic dislocation isn’t surprising after all.

data.title: Gold On Fire
data.description:
It's getting crazy now, and it's obvious gold is telling us something.

It began last night, as the price of gold began to take off in overseas trading.

The chatter on X alerted those of us trying to get away from the markets for a few hours to what was going on. I took a moment from my BBQ to check the price. It was up about $40, no news.

It seemed that Asia, again, wanted gold.

Being a gold bug veteran of many a 2x4 upside the head delivered by Mr. Market, I restrained my enthusiasm until after the typical smash-down time on the New York opening.

Give them credit - they tried - but nothing could stop this train today:

After beating back a couple of bear attacks, gold took off with little remaining resistance. As I write, it’s up about $108 on the day. Silver can’t keep up to this rocket ship, but is still up very nicely.

Frankly, this move is somewhat worrisome. After a brief correction earlier this month, gold’s overheated status cooled off, with its RSI dipping back below 70. But it’s right back in the stratosphere after the remarkable surge in recent sessions.

Similarly worrisome, gold is catching a buzz in the mainstream, as illustrated by this text my daughter received this morning from one of her law school friends:

While I appreciate the shoutout... and my investment portfolio is indeed shining right now... this is the sort of thing that has my contrarian Spidey senses tingling. Is this kind of mainstream recognition a warning that the move in gold has peaked?

I don’t think so, at least for the longer term.

First, if almost everyone you hang out with and talk to is a contrarian, it’s hard to figure out what is truly a contrarian view. Pulling back and looking at the big picture, I believe what we’re seeing right now is merely the start of a long-lasting move into metals and the miners from mainstream investors.

There’s lots more to go before this bull market ends.

Second, I think gold is telling us something now. As I’ve written here recently, all investment markets are predictive mechanisms, but I believe gold is, along with bonds, the most sensitive of all.

It soared after the Covid shutdown, not because inflation was soaring but because it was discounting the inflation to come as a result of the rescue efforts from the Fed and other central banks. Pundits bemoaned gold’s lack of reaction when that inflation finally emerged, but gold had already reacted long before.

Today, gold is once again warning us of severe repercussions ahead... and perhaps a reordering of the global monetary system.

What these repercussions are will be revealed in time, although I believe they will involve an economic slowdown, another monetary rescue from the Fed, and the resulting inflationary effects.

In the meantime, despite gold’s rocket-like surge, gold stocks have managed to dramatically outperform the metal. Again, that’s clear in the chart below of the GDX/gold ratio, updated from when I showed it to you on Monday:

More to the point, the stocks in our Gold Newsletter portfolio are just beginning to take off.

I won’t belabor the point, except to say this: This train is leaving the station, and if you’re not on board, you’re going to miss one of the best investing opportunities of your career.
